name: Confirmation Bias
definition: We tend to search for, interpret, and recall information that confirms our existing beliefs.
description: Confirmation bias leads people to favor information that supports what they already think, while ignoring or downplaying evidence that contradicts it.
everydayTip: Before forming an opinion, actively seek out one source that disagrees with you.

name: Anchoring Bias
definition: We rely too heavily on the first piece of information offered (the 'anchor') when making decisions.
description: Once an anchor is set, later judgments are made by adjusting away from it, but the adjustment is usually insufficient.
everydayTip: When negotiating, try to set your own reference point first instead of reacting to the other side's opening number.

name: Availability Heuristic
definition: We judge the likelihood of events based on how easily examples come to mind.
description: Vivid, recent, or emotionally charged events are recalled more easily, so we overestimate how common they are.
everydayTip: Check statistics instead of relying on memorable stories when estimating risk.

name: Fundamental Attribution Error
definition: We judge others' behavior as caused by their character, while excusing our own behavior as caused by circumstances.
description: When explaining someone else's actions we overweight personality traits and underweight situational factors, but we do the opposite for ourselves.
everydayTip: Before judging someone's behavior, ask what situational pressures might explain it.

name: Self-serving Bias
definition: We attribute our successes to our own abilities and our failures to external circumstances.
description: This bias protects self-esteem by crediting ourselves for good outcomes while blaming outside factors for bad ones.
everydayTip: When something goes wrong, ask honestly what part, if any, was within your control.

name: Sunk Cost Fallacy
definition: We continue investing in something because of what we've already invested, rather than evaluating future value.
description: Past costs (time, money, effort) that cannot be recovered still influence decisions, even though only future costs and benefits should matter.
everydayTip: Ask: 'if I hadn't invested anything yet, would I still choose to start this today?'

name: Negativity Bias
definition: We give more psychological weight to negative experiences than to positive ones of equal intensity.
description: Bad events, criticism, or emotions tend to have a bigger impact on our mood, memory, and decisions than good ones.
everydayTip: When reviewing feedback, deliberately count the positive points as well as the negative ones.

name: Halo Effect
definition: Our overall impression of a person influences how we judge their specific traits, so one positive trait makes us assume other positive traits.
description: If someone is attractive, likeable, or competent in one area, we tend to assume they're good in unrelated areas too.
everydayTip: Evaluate people's specific skills and claims separately from your overall impression of them.

name: Horn Effect
definition: One negative trait about a person leads us to assume other negative traits, the opposite of the halo effect.
description: A single flaw or bad first impression can unfairly color our judgment of someone's other, unrelated qualities.
everydayTip: When you notice a negative first impression, pause and check if you're generalizing unfairly.

name: Survivorship Bias
definition: We focus on the people or things that 'survived' a process, while overlooking those that didn't, skewing our conclusions.
description: Because failures are often invisible or unreported, we draw conclusions from an incomplete, success-only sample.
everydayTip: When hearing a success story, actively ask how many people tried the same thing and failed.

name: Hindsight Bias
definition: After an event happens, we tend to believe we 'knew it all along,' even if we couldn't have predicted it beforehand.
description: Once we know the outcome, our memory of our own earlier predictions shifts to seem more accurate than they really were.
everydayTip: Write predictions down beforehand so you can honestly check them against what you actually said.

name: False Consensus Effect
definition: We overestimate how much other people share our beliefs, opinions, and behaviors.
description: This bias makes our own views feel more common or 'normal' than they actually are in the wider population.
everydayTip: Before assuming your opinion is the majority view, ask how you would actually know that.

name: In-group Bias
definition: We favor members of our own group over members of other groups, often unconsciously.
description: This bias leads to more trust, generosity, and positive judgment toward people we see as 'us' compared to 'them.'
everydayTip: When evaluating people from a different group, ask if you'd judge the same behavior differently coming from your own group.

name: Out-group Homogeneity Bias
definition: We tend to see members of other groups as more similar to each other than they really are, while seeing our own group as diverse.
description: This bias leads to stereotyping, since we assume 'they're all the same' about outsiders while recognizing individuality within our own group.
everydayTip: When you catch yourself thinking 'they're all like that,' pause and ask if you'd say the same about your own group.

name: Framing Effect
definition: Our decisions are influenced by how information is presented (framed), even when the underlying facts are the same.
description: The same statistic or choice can lead to different decisions depending on whether it's framed in terms of gains or losses.
everydayTip: Try restating a choice in the opposite frame (gain vs. loss) before deciding.

name: Loss Aversion
definition: The pain of losing something feels psychologically stronger than the pleasure of gaining something of equal value.
description: This asymmetry means people often make decisions to avoid losses rather than to pursue equivalent gains.
everydayTip: When facing a risky decision, ask whether you're avoiding a loss out of proportion to what you'd actually miss.

name: Optimism Bias
definition: We tend to overestimate the likelihood of positive events happening to us and underestimate the likelihood of negative ones.
description: This bias makes people believe they are less likely than others to experience negative outcomes like illness, accidents, or failure.
everydayTip: When planning, ask what would happen if things went worse than expected, not just as planned.

name: Status Quo Bias
definition: We prefer things to stay the same, and treat the current state as a reasonable default, even when change might be better.
description: This bias makes people resist switching to new options, defaulting instead to whatever is already in place.
everydayTip: When comparing the current option to a new one, imagine you were choosing between them fresh, with no history.

name: Dunning-Kruger Effect
definition: People with limited knowledge or skill in an area tend to overestimate their own competence.
description: A little knowledge can create unearned confidence, while true experts are often more aware of what they don't know.
everydayTip: Before feeling confident about a new skill, ask an actual expert to check your assumptions.

name: Authority Bias
definition: We tend to attribute greater accuracy and trust to the opinions of an authority figure, regardless of the actual content.
description: A person's title, uniform, or perceived status can make their claims seem more credible, even without supporting evidence.
everydayTip: Evaluate a claim by its evidence, then separately consider whether the source's authority is actually relevant to that claim.

name: Planning Fallacy
definition: We routinely underestimate how long tasks will take and overestimate how smoothly they will go.
description: Even with past experience of delays, we often believe this time will be different and plan too optimistically.
everydayTip: When estimating time, look at how long similar tasks actually took — not how long you hoped they would take.

name: Recency Bias
definition: We give more weight to recent events than to older ones, even when the older pattern matters more.
description: The latest example feels freshest and can overshadow a longer track record of evidence.
everydayTip: Before deciding, ask what the full history shows — not just the last week.

name: Bandwagon Effect
definition: We are more likely to adopt a belief or behaviour because many others are doing it.
description: Popularity can feel like proof, even when we have not checked the reasons ourselves.
everydayTip: Before joining in, ask whether you would still choose this if nobody else were doing it.

name: Mere Exposure Effect
definition: We tend to like things more simply because we have encountered them before.
description: Familiarity can create warmth and preference, even without new reasons to prefer something.
everydayTip: Notice when you like something mainly because it feels familiar — not because you have compared it fairly.

name: Endowment Effect
definition: We value things more highly simply because we already own them.
description: Ownership changes how much something feels worth, compared with an identical item we do not own.
everydayTip: When selling or keeping something, ask what you would pay for it today if you did not already have it.

name: Gambler's Fallacy
definition: We expect random events to balance out in the short run, as if past outcomes change future odds.
description: Independent random events do not 'make up' for earlier results, but it can feel like they should.
everydayTip: Ask whether each new event is truly independent — or whether you are expecting a pattern that randomness does not owe you.

name: Clustering Illusion
definition: We see meaningful patterns in small sets of random or unrelated events.
description: A short streak of similar outcomes can feel like a signal, even when it is just noise.
everydayTip: Before calling something a pattern, ask how much data you really have.

name: Representativeness Heuristic
definition: We judge how likely something is by how much it resembles a stereotype, instead of using actual odds.
description: A good story or familiar type can feel more probable than the base rate suggests.
everydayTip: When a description feels like a perfect fit, check the real frequency before deciding.

name: Base Rate Neglect
definition: We ignore general base-rate information and focus on specific, vivid details instead.
description: A striking example can overpower the broader statistic about how common something really is.
everydayTip: Start with how often something happens in general, then adjust — not the other way around.

name: Peak-End Rule
definition: We judge an experience mainly by its most intense moment and how it ended, not the whole duration.
description: A strong ending or peak moment can reshape memory more than hours of ordinary experience.
everydayTip: When recalling an event, ask what happened in the middle — not only the highlight or the finish.

name: Choice-Supportive Bias
definition: After making a choice, we remember the chosen option more favourably and downplay its downsides.
description: Once we commit, our memory and judgment tilt toward making that choice look wiser.
everydayTip: After buying or deciding, ask what you would say to a friend making the same choice fresh today.

name: Illusion of Control
definition: We overestimate how much influence we have over outcomes that are mostly chance or outside our control.
description: Small actions can feel like they are steering big results, even when they are not.
everydayTip: Ask which parts of the outcome you can actually affect — and which parts are luck or other people.

name: Spotlight Effect
definition: We overestimate how much other people notice our appearance, mistakes, or behaviour.
description: We are each at the centre of our own attention, but others are usually paying far less attention to us.
everydayTip: When you feel painfully visible, ask what you would think if a friend did the same thing.

name: Cognitive Dissonance
definition: When our actions clash with our beliefs, we often change our beliefs or excuses rather than face the clash.
description: Mental discomfort pushes us to justify, minimise, or reframe what we did so it fits who we think we are.
everydayTip: When you catch yourself rationalising, ask what belief your action is bumping into.

name: Belief Perseverance
definition: We keep believing something even after the evidence supporting it has been weakened or removed.
description: Once a belief takes hold, we can defend it with new reasons after the original reasons fall away.
everydayTip: Ask what evidence would change your mind — and whether you are still using it honestly.

name: Normalcy Bias
definition: We underestimate the chance of disruption and assume things will keep going as they usually do.
description: Familiar routines feel stable, so warnings or unusual signs can be brushed aside too easily.
everydayTip: When something feels 'unthinkable,' ask what you would do if it happened anyway.

name: Curse of Knowledge
definition: Once we know something, we find it hard to imagine not knowing it.
description: Experts and experienced people can explain in ways that skip steps obvious to them but not to beginners.
everydayTip: When explaining, ask what confused you when you first learned this.

name: Illusory Correlation
definition: We perceive a link between two things when none exists, or see a stronger link than the data supports.
description: Memorable pairings can feel like cause and effect, especially when we already expect a connection.
everydayTip: Before blaming one thing for another, ask how often the effect happens without the cause.

name: Conjunction Fallacy
definition: We judge a detailed, specific story as more likely than a broader, more general one.
description: Extra detail can make a scenario feel more believable, even though specifics always lower the true odds.
everydayTip: Ask whether each added detail makes the story more vivid — or actually more probable.

name: Illusory Truth Effect
definition: Repeated statements feel more true, even when we know we should check them.
description: Familiar wording creates a sense of accuracy, whether or not the claim has been verified.
everydayTip: Treat familiarity as a reason to pause — not as proof something is correct.

name: Actor-Observer Bias
definition: We explain other people's actions by their character, but our own by the situation.
description: The same kind of behaviour gets different stories depending on whether we or someone else did it.
everydayTip: When judging someone else, ask what situation might explain it — the way you would for yourself.

name: Bias Blind Spot
definition: We notice biases in others more easily than in ourselves.
description: It can feel obvious that other people are skewed, while our own view still feels objective.
everydayTip: When you spot a bias in someone else, ask where the same pattern might show up in you.

name: Naive Realism
definition: We believe we see reality directly and that reasonable people should agree with us.
description: Our own perspective can feel like plain fact, making disagreement seem like bias or bad faith in others.
everydayTip: When someone disagrees, ask what they might be seeing that you are not.

name: Just-World Hypothesis
definition: We assume people generally get what they deserve, and search for reasons bad outcomes were earned.
description: The idea of a fair world can make us blame victims or downplay luck and circumstance.
everydayTip: When something bad happens to someone, notice if you are looking for a reason they 'must have' deserved it.

name: Hostile Attribution Bias
definition: We interpret ambiguous actions from others as intentionally hostile or unkind.
description: A neutral tone, delay, or mistake can be read as personal attack when the intent is unclear.
everydayTip: Before assuming someone meant harm, ask what neutral explanation would also fit.

name: Egocentric Bias
definition: We remember our own role in events as larger and more central than it was.
description: Our contribution, importance, or effort can loom larger in memory than in the shared reality.
everydayTip: When recalling a group effort, ask what others would say they contributed.

name: Illusion of Transparency
definition: We overestimate how clearly others can read our emotions and thoughts.
description: What feels obvious inside can be nearly invisible outside, but we often assume people can tell.
everydayTip: If something matters, say it plainly — do not assume your feelings are showing clearly.

name: Hyperbolic Discounting
definition: We prefer smaller rewards now over larger rewards later, more steeply than calm planning would suggest.
description: The immediate option feels disproportionately attractive compared with a better long-term choice.
everydayTip: Ask what future-you would prefer — not only what right-now-you wants.

name: Ambiguity Effect
definition: We avoid options when the odds of a good outcome are unclear, even if they might be favourable.
description: Known risks can feel safer than unknown ones, so we may stick with the familiar gamble.
everydayTip: When you avoid something because it feels uncertain, ask whether the known option is truly better.

name: Identifiable Victim Effect
definition: We respond more strongly to one identifiable person in need than to large, statistical groups.
description: A single story with a face and name can move us more than numbers describing many people.
everydayTip: Notice when one story is pulling your attention away from the broader scale of a problem.

name: Restraint Bias
definition: We overestimate our ability to resist temptation in the moment.
description: We predict strong self-control in advance, then struggle when the temptation is actually in front of us.
everydayTip: Plan for the moment of temptation, not the moment of planning.

name: Reactance
definition: When we feel our freedom is threatened, we push back — sometimes by doing the opposite.
description: Being told what to do can spark resistance, even when the advice is reasonable.
everydayTip: Notice when you are saying no mainly because someone told you yes — or the reverse.

name: Ostrich Effect
definition: We avoid information that might force us to face an uncomfortable problem.
description: Not looking can feel like relief, even though the issue remains and may grow worse unseen.
everydayTip: If you are avoiding a number, email, or conversation, ask what you are afraid it will confirm.

name: Zero-Sum Bias
definition: We treat situations as if one person's gain must mean another person's loss, even when both could benefit.
description: Success, praise, or opportunity can feel like a fixed pie, making cooperation harder to see.
everydayTip: When someone else wins, ask whether anything actually came off your plate.

name: Moral Luck
definition: We judge the morality of an action partly by how lucky or unlucky the outcome was.
description: The same choice can look wise or reckless depending on results that were not fully in the person's control.
everydayTip: Try judging the decision separately from how lucky the outcome turned out to be.

name: Texas Sharpshooter Fallacy
definition: We notice a cluster first, then draw a target around it as if we predicted it.
description: Patterns can be invented after the fact, making random data look meaningful.
everydayTip: Ask whether you are finding a pattern because you looked for it — or because you predicted it beforehand.

name: Overconfidence Effect
definition: We are more certain of our judgments than our accuracy usually supports.
description: Confidence can feel like proof, even when we are wrong surprisingly often.
everydayTip: When you feel sure, ask what would make you only 70% sure instead of 100%.

name: Pro-Innovation Bias
definition: We assume a new tool or change will help more than it does, and overlook its limits.
description: Novelty can feel like progress by itself, even before evidence shows it works better.
everydayTip: Ask what problem the new thing solves — and what it might break.

name: Semmelweis Reflex
definition: We reject new evidence or ideas because they challenge established norms or beliefs.
description: Familiar ways of doing things can feel safer than unfamiliar evidence, even when the evidence is strong.
everydayTip: When a new idea feels wrong mainly because it is new, separate that feeling from whether it is actually wrong.

name: Risk Compensation
definition: We take more risks when we feel safer, partly offsetting the protection we gained.
description: Extra safety can lead to looser behaviour, so the overall risk may not fall as much as expected.
everydayTip: When a safety measure makes you feel protected, ask whether you are relaxing in ways that bring risk back.
